## Summary:
Neural networks are the main building blocks of Machine leraning with Maths calculations. Which helps how transformers can predict AI the next word. I learnt mostly about how neurnal networks get connected with each other having multiple layers, how they need to calculate and prioritizing the neuron in the network based on score using Sigmod. Transformers are the building blocks of AI.

## Key concepts:
- Each pixels grid is scale of 0-1.
- Each image of 28x28 is 784 pixels converts to neurons.
- Which each neuron has a weighed sum along with bias - w1*a1 + b1.
- Weighted sum is like dark to light representation of each pixels in a grid like 0.01 to 0.99.
- Complete first layer of neurons are wrapped by sigmoid, now using Relu.

## Suprised:
- Simple and small image has 28x28 pixels.
- Use of math matrices, image pixels are represented as matrices and uses matrix calculations.
- Transformers are from Google, which plays a key role for OpenAI or other LLMs.

Final Summary:
- Complete flow: Text comes in → split into tokens → each token converted to a vector (embedding) → vectors passed through attention mechanism (finds relationships between tokens) → passed through feedforward block (Linear → ReLU → Linear) → repeat through N transformer blocks → final output predicts next token probability → highest probability token is selected → repeat until done.
- Backpropagation: it only happens during training — not during inference. When you send a prompt to an LLM, the model is already trained. Weights are frozen. No backpropagation happens at all during your conversation. Backpropagation is how the model learned — not how it responds. The model is just doing maths forward, not learning. 
how does the model know when to stop?
- The answer has two parts.
"""
Part 1 — Transformers do not work one at a time sequentially per token. All transformer blocks run on the full input in one forward pass. The prompt goes through Block 1, then Block 2, then Block 3... all the way through all N blocks — in sequence but in one generation step. It does not stop at Block 5 and send a partial answer. All blocks complete, then one token is predicted.
Part 2 — Stopping is controlled by a special token. During training the model learned that a special token called <EOS> (End of Sentence) signals completion. When the model's probability distribution outputs <EOS> as the most likely next token, generation stops. There is also a max_tokens parameter you can set in the API — a hard cutoff regardless of EOS.
So the correct mental model is this:
Prompt enters
→ All transformer blocks process it fully (one forward pass)
→ Output: probability distribution over all possible next tokens
→ Pick highest probability token
→ Append to sequence
→ Run all blocks again on the extended sequence
→ Repeat until EOS token or max_tokens reached
Each token generation = one complete pass through ALL transformer blocks. Not one block per token.
"""
